en.extract:
The red-vented bulbul (Pycnonotus cafer) is a member of the bulbul family of passerines. It is a resident breeder across South Asia extending east to Burma. It has been introduced in many other parts of the world and has established itself in Argentina, Tonga and Fiji, as well as parts of Samoa, USA and Cook Islands. It is included in the list of the world's 100 worst invasive alien species. In New Zealand they were the target of a successful extermination campaign in 1955, however they are still sporadically reported.


Taxonomy and systematics
In 1760 the French zoologist Mathurin Jacques Brisson included a description of the red-vented bulbul in his Ornithologie based on a specimen that he mistakenly believed had been collected from the Cape of Good Hope in South Africa. He used the French name Le merle hupé du Cap de Bonne Espérance and the Latin Merula Cristata Capitis Bonae Spei. Although Brisson coined Latin names, these do not conform to the binomial system and are not recognised by the International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature. When in 1766 the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus updated his Systema Naturae for the twelfth edition, he added 240 species that had been previously described by Brisson. One of these was the red-vented bulbul. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the binomial name Turdus cafer and cited Brisson's work. The red-vented bulbul does not occur in Africa. The type location was later changed to Sri Lanka and then in 1952 designated as Pondicherry in India by the German naturalist Erwin Stresemann. The specific epithet cafer is Neo-Latin for South Africa. This species is now placed in the genus Pycnonotus that was introduced by the German zoologist Friedrich Boie in 1826.
Two formerly designated races, P. c. nigropileus in southern Burma and P. c. burmanicus of northern Burma, are now considered as hybrids.


Subspecies
Eight subspecies are recognized:

Central Indian red-vented bulbul (P. c. humayuni) - Deignan, 1951: Found in south-eastern Pakistan, north-western and north-central India
Punjab red-vented bulbul (P. c. intermedius) - Blyth, 1846: Originally described as a separate species. Found in Kashmir and Kohat down to the Salt Range and along the western Himalayas to Kumaon.
P. c. bengalensis - Blyth, 1845: Originally described as a separate species. Found in the central and eastern Himalayas from Nepal to Assam, north-eastern India and Bangladesh
P. c. stanfordi - Deignan, 1949: Found in northern Burma and south-western China
P. c. melanchimus - Deignan, 1949: Found in south-central Burma and northern Thailand
P. c. wetmorei - Deignan, 1960: Found in eastern India
P. c. saturatus - (Whistler & Kinnear, 1932): Originally described as a separate species Stelgidocichla latirostris saturata (Mearns 1914). Found in north-eastern India
P. c. cafer - (Linnaeus, 1766): Found in southern India
P. c. haemorrhousus - (Gmelin, JF, 1789): Found in Sri Lanka


Description
The red-vented bulbul is easily identified by its short crest giving the head a squarish appearance. The body is dark brown with a scaly pattern while the head is darker or black. The rump is white while the vent is red. The bird is about 20 cm in length, with a long black tail, tipped in white. The Himalayan races have a more prominent crest and are more streaked on the underside. The race intermedius of the Western Himalayas has a black hood extending to the mid-breast. The population bengalensis of the Central and Eastern Himalayas and the Gangetic plain has a dark hood, lacks the scale-like pattern on the underside and instead has dark streaks on the paler lower belly. Race stanfordi of the South Assam hills is similar to intermedius. The desert race humayuni has a paler brown mantle. The nominate race cafer is found in Peninsular India. The Northeast Indian race wetmorei is between cafer, humayuni and bengalensis. The Sri Lankan race haemorrhous (=haemorrhousus) has a dark mantle with narrow pale edges. Race humayuni is known to hybridize with Pycnonotus leucogenys and these hybrids were once described as a subspecies magrathi marked by their pale rumps and yellow-orange or pink vents. In eastern Myanmar there is some natural hybridization with Pycnonotus aurigaster.
Sexes are similar in plumage, but young birds are duller than adults. The typical call has been transcribed as ginger beer, but a number of sharp single note calls sounding like pick are also produced. Their alarm calls are usually responded to and heeded by many other species of bird.
Melanistic as well as leucistic individuals have been noted. An individual with aberrant colour form was observed in Bhavans College Campus, Andheri, Mumbai.


Distribution and habitat
This is a bird of dry scrub, open forest, plains and cultivated lands. In its native range it is rarely found in mature forests. A study based on 54 localities in India concluded that vegetation is the single most important factor that determines the distribution of the species.
Red-vented bulbuls were introduced into Fiji in 1903 by indentured labourers from India, becoming widespread. They were introduced to Tonga in 1943 and became common on Samoa by 1957. They became established on the Tongan islands of Tongatapu and Niuafo'ou. They were introduced into Melbourne around 1917 but were not seen after 1942. They established in Auckland in the 1950s but were exterminated and another wild population was detected and exterminated in 2006. In 2013 more were found, and authorities offered a $1000 reward for information that led to a bird's capture. They prefer dry lowland habitat in these regions. They were first observed breeding on the Canary Islands in 2018. They are considered as pests because of their habit of damaging fruit crops. Methiocarb and ziram have been used to protect cultivated Dendrobium orchids in Hawaii from damage by these birds; however, they learn to avoid the repellent chemicals. They can also disperse the seeds of invasive plants like Lantana camara and Miconia calvescens.
P. cafer is invasive in New Caledonia. Thibault et al. (2018) finds this species to be pushing out native species but not other introduced species.


Behaviour and ecology

Red-vented bulbuls feed on fruits, petals of flowers, nectar, insects and occasionally house geckos (Hemidactylus flaviviridis). They have also been seen feeding on the leaves of Medicago sativa.
Red-vented bulbuls build their nests in bushes at a height of around 2–3 m (6.6–9.8 ft). Nests are occasionally built inside houses or in a hole in a mud bank. In one instance, a nest was found on a floating mat of Water hyacinth leaves and another observer noted a pair nesting inside a regularly used bus. Nests in tree cavities have also been noted. Nesting in safe cavities of residential buildings has also been noted.
They breed from June to September and lay two or three eggs in a typical clutch. The eggs are pale-pinkish with spots of dark red color which are more dense at the broad end. However, in one instance, breeding has also been observed in February in Tamil Nadu. They are capable of having multiple clutches in a year. Nests are small flat cups made of small dry twigs and spider web, but sometimes making use of metal wires. The eggs hatch after about 14 days. Both parents feed the chicks and on feeding trips wait for the young to excrete, swallowing the faecal sacs produced for the first few days when the bacterial level is minimum. Later they carry the faecal sacs and dump them elsewhere. The pied crested cuckoo is a brood parasite of this species. Fires, heavy rains and predators are the main causes of fledgling mortality in scrub habitats in southern India.

Their vocalizations are usually stereotyped and they call throughout the year. However, a number of distinct call types have been identified including roosting, begging, greeting, flight and two kinds of alarm calls.
They are important dispersers of seeds of plants such as Carissa spinarum.
The red-vented bulbul was among the first animals other than humans that was found to be incapable of synthesizing vitamin C. However, a large number of other birds were later found to likewise lack the ability to synthesize vitamin C.
Like most birds, these bulbuls are hosts to coccidian blood parasites (Isospora sp.) while some bird lice such as Menacanthus guldum (Ansari 1951 Proc. Natl. Inst. Sci. India 17:40) have been described as ectoparasites.
Along with red-whiskered bulbuls this species has led to changes in the population dynamics of butterfly morphs on the island of Oahu in Hawaii. Here the population of white morphs of the Danaus plexippus butterfly has risen over a period of 20 years due to predation of the orange morphs by these bulbuls.


In culture
In 19th-century India these birds were frequently kept as cage pets and for fighting especially in the Carnatic region. They would be held on the finger with a thread attached and when they fought they would seize the red feathers of the opponents.

Indians frequently tame it and carry it about the bazaars, tied with a string to the finger or to a little crutched perch, which is often made of precious metals or jade; while there are few Europeans who do not recollect Eha's immortal phrase anent the red patch in the seat of its trousers.In the state of Assam, India, (the Bulbul (বুলবুলী) bird as it is known in Assamese), the male birds were held captive for a few days and were engaged in fights as a spectator sport in the Bihu festival during the Ahom rule. This practice was banned in January 2016.


References

[[Rajasthan]], India PycnonotusMagrathiKeulemans.jpg|''P. leucogenys'' x ''P. c. humayuni'' hybrid (''magrathi'') Red vented Bulbul I IMG 6303.jpg|Underside of ''P. c. bengalensis'' Red-vented bulbul (Pycnonotus cafer haemorrhousus) vent.jpg|''P. c. haemorrhousus'' showing vent, Sri Lanka. ==Description== The red-vented bulbul is easily identified by its short crest giving the head a squarish appearance. The body is dark brown with a scaly pattern while the head is darker or black. The rump is white while the vent is red. The bird is about 20 cm in length, with a long black tail, tipped in white. The Himalayan races have a more prominent crest and are more streaked on the underside. The race ''intermedius'' of the Western Himalayas has a black hood extending to the mid-breast. The population ''bengalensis'' of the Central and Eastern Himalayas and the Gangetic plain has a dark hood, lacks the scale-like pattern on the underside and instead has dark streaks on the paler lower belly. Race ''stanfordi'' of the South Assam hills is similar to ''intermedius''. The desert race ''humayuni'' has a paler brown mantle. The nominate race ''cafer'' is found in Peninsular India. The Northeast Indian race ''wetmorei'' is between ''cafer'', ''humayuni'' and ''bengalensis''. The Sri Lankan race ''haemorrhous'' (=''haemorrhousus'') has a dark mantle with narrow pale edges. Race ''humayuni'' is known to hybridize with ''[[Pycnonotus leucogenys]]'' and these hybrids were once described as a subspecies ''magrathi'' marked by their pale rumps and yellow-orange or pink vents.{{cite journal|year=1959|title= Hybridization in some Indian Bulbuls ''Pycnonotus cafer'' x ''P. leucogenys''|journal=Ibis|volume= 101|issue=2|pages=177–182|doi= 10.1111/j.1474-919X.1959.tb02373.x|author1=Sibley, CB|author2=Short, LL}} In eastern Myanmar there is some natural hybridization with ''[[Pycnonotus aurigaster]]''.{{cite book|author1=Rasmussen PC |author2=Anderton, JC |name-list-style=amp |isbn=978-84-87334-67-2 |year=2005|title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide|publisher=Smithsonian Institution and Lynx Edicions|page=338}}{{cite journal|title=A Note on ''Molpastes magrathi'' Whitehead| journal= Ibis| volume=51| issue=2| pages= 302–304 | year= 1909| author=Sharpe, R B|url= https://archive.org/stream/ibis39brit#page/302/mode/1up| doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1909.tb05264.x}} Sexes are similar in plumage, but young birds are duller than adults. The typical call has been transcribed as ''ginger beer'', but a number of sharp single note calls sounding like ''pick'' are also produced. Their alarm calls are usually responded to and heeded by many other species of bird. A study based on 54 localities in India concluded that vegetation is the single most important factor that determines the distribution of the species.{{cite book|author=Vijayan, VS|year=1975|title= The ecological isolation of Bulbuls (Pycnonotidae) with special reference to ''Pycnonotus cafer cafer'' and ''P. luteolus luteolus'' at Point Calimere, Tamil Nadu. Ph.D. Thesis, University of Bombay}} Red-vented bulbuls were introduced into Fiji in 1903 by indentured labourers from India, becoming widespread. They were introduced to Tonga in 1943 and became common on Samoa by 1957.{{cite journal|author=Watling, D|year=1978|title=Observations on the naturalized distribution of the Red-vented Bulbul in Africa, with special reference to the Fiji islands|url=http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_25-1978/Notornis_25_2.pdf|journal=Notornis|volume=25|pages=109–117|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081016125847/http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_25-1978/Notornis_25_2.pdf|archive-date=2008-10-16}} They became established on the Tongan islands of Tongatapu and Niuafo'ou. They were introduced into Melbourne around 1917 but were not seen after 1942.{{cite book |title=Introduced Birds of the World: The worldwide history, distribution and influence of birds introduced to new environments |last=Long |first=John L. |year=1981 |publisher=Reed |location=Terrey Hills, Sydney |isbn=978-0-589-50260-7|page=300}} They established in Auckland in the 1950s but were exterminated{{cite journal|author=Gill, BJ|author2=GR Hunt|author3=S Sirgouant|name-list-style=amp|title=Red-vented Bulbuls (''Pycnonotus cafer'') in New Caledonia.|pages=214–215|url=http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_42-1995/Notornis_42_3_214.pdf|journal=Notornis|volume=42|issue=3|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081018130012/http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_42-1995/Notornis_42_3_214.pdf|archive-date=2008-10-18}} and another wild population was detected and exterminated in 2006.{{cite web |title=Red Vented Bulbul, Ministry for Primary Industry Pests and Diseases |url=http://www.biosecurity.govt.nz/pests/red-vented-bulbul |access-date=2013-07-25 |archive-date=2008-10-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081014021111/http://biosecurity.govt.nz/pests/red-vented-bulbul }} In 2013 more were found, and authorities offered a $1000 reward for information that led to a bird's capture.[http://www.3news.co.nz/Labour-angry-at-1000-angry-bird-reward/tabid/1607/articleID/321411/Default.aspx#.UoVgOuHIhnE Labour angry at $1000 angry bird reward]. ''3 News NZ''. 15 November 2013. They prefer dry lowland habitat in these regions.{{cite journal|year=1984|title=Differential expansion and population growth of bulbuls in Hawaii|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v096n04/p0647-p0655.pdf|issue=4 |journal= Wilson Bulletin|volume=96|pages=647–655|author1=Williams, R.N|author2=Giddings, L.V}}{{cite journal|journal=Notornis|year=2005|volume=52|pages=16–20|title=The further spread of introduced birds in Samoa|author1=McAllan, Ian AW|author2=Hobcroft, D.|name-list-style=amp|url=http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_52-2005/Notornis_52_1_16.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081018071729/http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_52-2005/Notornis_52_1_16.pdf|archive-date=2008-10-18}} They were first observed breeding on the Canary Islands in 2018.{{cite journal|journal=BioInvasions Records|year=2019|volume=8|issue=4|pages=947–952|title=The Red-vented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linnaeus, 1766) – a new invasive bird species breeding in Europe|author1= Nowakowski JJ|author2= Dulisz B|name-list-style=amp|url=https://www.reabic.net/journals/bir/2019/4/BIR_2019_Nowakowski_Dulisz.pdf|doi=10.3391/bir.2019.8.4.24|doi-access=free}} They are considered as pests because of their habit of damaging fruit crops. [[Methiocarb]] and [[ziram]] have been used to protect cultivated ''Dendrobium'' orchids in Hawaii from damage by these birds; however, they learn to avoid the repellent chemicals.{{cite journal|title=Evaluation of methiocarb, ziram, and methyl anthranilate as bird repellents applied to Dendrobium orchids|journal=Wildl. Soc. Bull.|volume=22|pages=633–638|year=1994|url=http://www.aphis.usda.gov/wildlife_damage/nwrc/publications/94pubs/94-9.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080922200556/http://www.aphis.usda.gov/wildlife_damage/nwrc/publications/94pubs/94-9.pdf|archive-date=September 22, 2008|author1=Cummings, JL|author2=Mason, J.R.|author3=Otis, D.L.|author4=Ohashi, J.E. Davis T.J.}} They can also disperse the seeds of invasive plants like ''[[Lantana camara]]'' and ''[[Miconia calvescens]]''.{{cite journal|url=http://hbs.bishopmuseum.org/pdf/melastome97.pdf|author1=Medeiros, A. C. |author2=Loope, L. L. |author3=Conant, P. |author4=McElvaney, S. |name-list-style=amp |year=1997|title=Status, ecology, and management of the invasive plant, Miconia calvescens DC (Melastomataceae) in the Hawaiian Islands|journal= Bishop Mus. Occas. Pap.|volume=48|pages=23–36}}{{cite journal|author=Berger, A. J.|year=1975|title= Red-whiskered and Red-vented Bulbuls on Oahu|journal=Elepaio |volume=36|pages=16–19}} ''P. cafer'' is invasive in [[New Caledonia]]. Thibault et al. (2018) finds this species to be pushing out native species but not other [[introduced species]].{{cite book | editor-last1=Downs | editor-first1=Colleen T. | editor-last2=Hart | editor-first2=Lorinda A. | title=Invasive birds global trends and impacts | publisher=[[Centre for Agriculture and Bioscience International]] (CABI) | publication-place=[[Wallingford, Oxfordshire]], UK [[Boston]] | year=2020 | isbn=978-1-78924-206-5 | oclc=1114281215 | pages=xvi+381}} {{ISBN|978-1-78924-207-2}}. {{ISBN|978-1-78924-208-9}}.{{rp|48}} ==Behaviour and ecology== [[File:Red-vented Bulbul in Tabakoshi March 2025 by Tisha Mukherjee 01.jpg|thumb| Red-vented Bulbul (''Pycnonotus cafer'') on Indian coral tree (''[[erythrina variegata]]'') in [[Darjeeling_district|Tabakoshi]], West Bengal, India.]] Red-vented bulbuls feed on fruits, petals of flowers,{{cite journal|author=Johnson, J. M.|year=1989|title=Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linne) eating petals of ''Magnolia''|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=86|issue=1|page=103|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48710447}} nectar, insects and occasionally house geckos ([[Hemidactylus flaviviridis]]).{{cite journal|author=Bharos, AMK|year=1999|title=Attempt by Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' to feed on a young House Gecko ''Hemidactylus flaviviridis''|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=96|issue=2|page=320|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48582999}}{{cite journal|author=Sharma, Satish Kumar|year=2000|title=Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' feeding on tail of House Gecko ''Hemidactylus flaviviridis''|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=97|issue=2|page=284|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48567718}}{{cite journal|author= Marathe, S|year=1989|title=Fly-catching bulbuls|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume= 29|issue=9&10|pages=10–11| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW29_910#page/n11/mode/1up}}{{cite journal|author= Balasubramanian, P |year=1991|title=Bulbuls feeding on the pulp of ''Cassia fistula'' pod in Pt. Calimere Wildlife Sanctuary, Tamil Nadu|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=88|issue=3|page=456|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48673892}}{{cite journal|author= Siromoney, Gift |year=1963|title= Bulbuls eating flowers|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume= 3 |issue=6|page= 12| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW3#page/n95/mode/1up/}}{{cite journal|author= Kumar, Satish |year=1995|title= Sugary exudate of Sorghum ''Sorghum bicolor'' as food of Large Grey Babbler ''Turdoides malcolmi'' (Sykes), Purplerumped Sunbird ''Nectarinia zeylonica'' (Linn.) and Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linnaeus)|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume= 92|issue=3|pages=421–422|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48613982}} They have also been seen feeding on the leaves of ''[[Medicago sativa]]''.{{cite journal|journal=Forktail|volume=17|year=2001|title=Foraging ecology of Red-vented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' in Haridwar, India|author1=Bhatt, Dinesh|author2=Anil Kumar|name-list-style=amp|pages=109–110|url=http://www.orientalbirdclub.org/publications/forktail/17pdfs/Bhatt-Bulbul.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081011022330/http://www.orientalbirdclub.org/publications/forktail/17pdfs/Bhatt-Bulbul.pdf|archive-date=2008-10-11}} Red-vented bulbuls build their nests in bushes at a height of around {{convert|2|–|3|m|ft|abbr=on}}. Nests are occasionally built inside houses{{cite journal|author=Dixit, D|year=1963|title=Notes on a case of the redvented bulbul, ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (linnaeus) nesting indoors|journal=Pavo|volume=1|issue=1|pages=19–31}}{{cite journal|author=Inglis, CM|year=1922|title= Curious site for nest of the Bengal Redvented Bulbul (''Molpastes haemarrhous bengalensis'')|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume= 28|issue=4|pages=1135–1136|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30348226}} or in a hole in a mud bank.{{cite journal|author=Lamba, BS|year=1976|title= Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' nesting in a hole in a mud bank|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=73|issue=2|page=395|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48293398}} In one instance, a nest was found on a floating mat of [[Water hyacinth]] leaves{{cite journal|author=Nanjappa, C|year=1989|title=An hitherto unrecorded nesting site of a Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linnaeus)|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume= 86|issue=1|page=102|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48710446}} and another observer noted a pair nesting inside a regularly used bus.{{cite journal |year=1998|title=Unusual nest location of Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linn.)|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=95|issue=1|page=116 |author1=Urfi, Abdul Jamil |author2=Jethua, Keshubha|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48604636}} Nests in tree cavities have also been noted.{{cite journal |year=1992|title= Additional nesting sites of Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linn.)|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=89|issue=2|page=257 |author1=Sivasubramanian, C |author2=Sundaramoorthy, T|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48732721}} Nesting in safe cavities of residential buildings has also been noted. They breed from June to September and lay two or three eggs in a typical clutch. The eggs are pale-pinkish with spots of dark red color which are more dense at the broad end.{{cite book|author=Jerdon, TC|title=The Bird of India Volume 2. Part 1.|year=1863|pages=93–96|url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsofindiabein21jerd#page/94/mode/1up/|publisher=Calcutta : Printed for the Author by the Military Orphan Press}} However, in one instance, breeding has also been observed in February in Tamil Nadu.{{citation needed|date=February 2022}} They are capable of having multiple clutches in a year. Nests are small flat cups made of small dry twigs and spider web, but sometimes making use of metal wires.{{cite journal|author=Lamba, BS|year=1968|title= Wire nests of Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linnaeus)|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=65|issue=1|pages=222–223|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48067717}} The eggs hatch after about 14 days.{{cite book|vauthors=Ali S, Ripley SD |year=1996|title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan|publisher=Oxford University Press|volume=6| edition=2nd| pages=85–92}} Both parents feed the chicks and on feeding trips wait for the young to excrete, swallowing the faecal sacs produced for the first few days when the bacterial level is minimum. Later they carry the faecal sacs and dump them elsewhere.{{cite journal|author=McCann, Charles|author-link=Charles McCann|year=1932|title=Notes on the nesting habits of the Red-vented Bulbul (''Molpastes cafer'')|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|issue=3|pages=680–681|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47858867|volume=35}} The [[Clamator jacobinus|pied crested cuckoo]] is a [[brood parasite]] of this species.{{cite journal|author=Tooth, EE|year=1902|title=A Pied-Crested Cuckoo's egg ''Coccystes jacobinus'' found in the nest of the Bengal Red-vented Bulbul ''Molpastes bengalensis''|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=14|issue=1|page=172|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30157811}} Fires, heavy rains and predators are the main causes of fledgling mortality in scrub habitats in southern India.{{cite journal|author1=Prabhakarachari, N |author2=Ravikumar, R |author3=Ramamurthi, R |year=1990|title=Ecobiology of redvented bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer cafer'' in a scrub jungle at Tirupati in Andhra Pradesh| journal=Journal of Ecobiology| volume=2|issue=1|pages=45–50}} [[File:Red-vented Bulbul (Pycnonotus cafer) nest in Anantgiri, AP W IMG 8900.jpg|thumb|left|Eggs inside the nest]] Their vocalizations are usually stereotyped and they call throughout the year. However, a number of distinct call types have been identified including roosting, begging, greeting, flight and two kinds of alarm calls.{{cite journal|author=Kumar, Anil|title=Acoustic communication in the Red-vented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer''|journal=Anais da Academia Brasileira de Ciências|year=2004|volume=76|issue=2|pages=350–358|url=http://www.scielo.br/pdf/aabc/v76n2/a24v76n2.pdf|doi=10.1590/S0001-37652004000200024|pmid=15258649|doi-access=free}} They are important dispersers of seeds of plants such as ''[[Carissa spinarum]]''.{{cite journal|title=Frugivory and seed dispersal of Carissa spinarum (L.) in a tropical deciduous forest of central India |author=Mishra, R.M. |author2=Gupta, P.|journal=Tropical Ecology|volume=46|issue=2|year=2005|pages=151–156|url=http://www.tropecol.com/pdf/open/PDF_46_2/151-156.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304032822/http://www.tropecol.com/pdf/open/PDF_46_2/151-156.pdf|url-status=usurped|archive-date=March 4, 2016}} The red-vented bulbul was among the first animals other than humans that was found to be incapable of synthesizing [[vitamin C]].{{cite journal|year=1958|title=Production of experimental scurvy in a bird species|journal=Nature|volume=182|pages=1689–1690|doi=10.1038/1821689b0|pmid=13622627|issue=4650|author1=Roy, RN|author2=Guha, BC|bibcode=1958Natur.182.1689R|s2cid=4145202}}{{cite book|title=Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry|author=Nelson|author2=Cox|name-list-style=amp|isbn=978-1-57259-931-4|year=2000|publisher=Worth Publ.|location=New York, NY|oclc=247815983|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/lehningerprincip01lehn}} However, a large number of other birds were later found to likewise lack the ability to synthesize vitamin C.{{cite journal | author = Gillespie D. S. | year = 1980 | title = Overview of Species Needing Dietary Vitamin C|jstor=20094480 | journal = The Journal of Zoo Animal Medicine | volume = 11 | issue = 3| pages = 88–91 | doi = 10.2307/20094480}} Like most birds, these bulbuls are hosts to [[coccidia]]n blood parasites (''[[Isospora]]'' sp.{{cite journal|hdl=1811/2942|volume=38|issue=3|pages=149–163|year=1938|journal=The Ohio Journal of Science|title=Avian Hosts of the Genus ''Isospora'' (Coccidiida)|author=Boughton, Donald}}) while some [[bird lice]] such as ''Menacanthus guldum'' (Ansari 1951 Proc. Natl. Inst. Sci. India 17:40) have been described as ectoparasites.{{cite journal|journal=J. Med. Entomol.|volume=14|issue=2|pages=207–220|year=1977|title=The ''Menacanthus'' (Mallophaga: Menoponidae) of the Passeriformes (Aves)|author=Price, Roger D.|doi=10.1093/jmedent/14.2.207|url=http://www.phthiraptera.org/Publications/0808.pdf|pmid=606822}}{{Dead link|date=August 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} Along with [[red-whiskered bulbul]]s this species has led to changes in the population dynamics of butterfly morphs on the island of Oahu in Hawaii. Here the population of white morphs of the ''[[Danaus plexippus]]'' butterfly has risen over a period of 20 years due to predation of the orange morphs by these bulbuls.{{cite journal|author1=Stimson, John |author2=Mark Berman |name-list-style=amp |title=Predator induced colour polymorphism in Danaus plexippus L. (Lepidoptera: Nymphalidae) in Hawaii|journal=Heredity|year=1990|volume =65|pages=401–406|doi=10.1038/hdy.1990.110|issue=3|doi-access=free}} ==In culture== In 19th-century India these birds were frequently kept as cage pets and for fighting especially in the [[Carnatic region]]. They would be held on the finger with a thread attached and when they fought they would seize the red feathers of the opponents. {{blockquote|Indians frequently tame it and carry it about the bazaars, tied with a string to the finger or to a little crutched perch, which is often made of precious metals or jade; while there are few Europeans who do not recollect [[Edward Hamilton Aitken|Eha]]'s immortal phrase anent the red patch in the seat of its trousers.|[[Hugh Whistler]]{{cite book|pages=[https://archive.org/details/popularhandbooko033226mbp/page/n103 68]–70|author=Whistler, Hugh|title=Popular Handbook of Indian Birds|url=https://archive.org/details/popularhandbooko033226mbp|edition=4th|publisher=Gurney & Jackson|year=1949}}}}In the state of [[Assam]], India, (the Bulbul (বুলবুলী) bird as it is known in [[Assamese language|Assamese]]), the male birds were held captive for a few days and were engaged in fights as a spectator sport{{rp|49}} in the [[Bihu festival]] during the Ahom rule. This practice was banned in January 2016.{{rp|49}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Further reading== * Bellary, Sadananda A; Desai, RN (2000) Unusual nesting activity of the Red-vented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'': two peculiar features. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' 40(6):83–84. * {{cite journal | author = Berger AJ | year = 1964 | title = The breeding seasons of Indian birds | journal = Pavo | volume = 2 | issue = 2| pages = 121–122}} * Chowdhury, SR; Bhattacharyya, SP (1989) Circannual variation in the alveolar histodynamics and secretory activity of the uropygial gland of the male Redvented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linnaeus). Pavo 27(1&2), 5–14. * Dasgupta, P; Bhattacharyya, SP (1988) Circannual changes in the testicular activity of the Red-vented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linnaeus). Pavo 26(1&2):37–48. * Deignan, HG (1949) Races of ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linnaeus) and ''P. aurigaster'' (Vieillot) in the Indo-Chinese subregion. J. Washington Acad. Sci. 39(8):273–279. * {{cite book|author = Desai, RN |year=1993| chapter=Two unusual nesting sites of the Redvented Bulbul (''Pycnonotus cafer'')|title= Bird Conservation: Strategies for the Nineties and Beyond| editor=Verghese, A| editor2=Sridhar, S| editor3=Chakravarthy, AK| publisher=Ornithological Society of India, Bangalore| page=190|chapter-url=https://archive.org/stream/BirdConservationStrategies/BirdConservationIndia#page/n217/mode/1up/search/pycnonotus}} * {{Cite journal|author= Desai, RN |year=1995| title=Incubation pattern in the Red-vented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' in relation to atmospheric temperature and the phase of development of eggs|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=35| issue=2| pages=35–36|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW35_2#page/n14/mode/1up/search/bulbul}} * {{cite journal|author= Desai, RN |year=1997| title=A case of an unusually delayed breeding activity of the Red-vented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer''|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=37| issue=2|page=27| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW37_2#page/n8/mode/1up/}} * {{cite journal | author = Dhamke Hemant A | year = 1997 | title = Possible feeding on an unhatched egg by young one of Redvented Bulbul (''Pycnonotus cafer'') |journal = J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. | volume = 94 | issue = 2| pages = 413–414|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48601775}} * {{cite journal | author = Ganguli U | year = 1963 | title = Some notes on the nesting and nest behaviour of a pair of redvented bulbuls |journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] | volume = 3 | issue = 10| pages = 2–5}} * {{cite journal | pmid = 7129090 | year = 1982 | last1 = Lal | first1 = P | last2 = Thapliyal | first2 = JP | title = Thyroid – gonad and thyroid – body weight relationship in the red-vented bulbul, Molpastes cafer | volume = 48 | issue = 1 | pages = 98–103 | journal = General and Comparative Endocrinology | doi = 10.1016/0016-6480(82)90042-9}} * {{cite journal |author1=Mummigatti UG |author2=Desai RN |author3=Desai Sarayu | year = 2001 | title = Some aspects of the developmental biology of the Red Vented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer''; The nestling's food and feeding pattern, and the feeding behaviour of their parents |journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] | volume = 41 | issue = 2| pages = 20–21}} * {{cite journal | author = Rana BD | year = 1976 | title = Drought food of ''Pycnonotus cafer'' and ''Psittacula krameri'' in the Rajasthan desert |journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] | volume = 16 | issue = 10| pages = 5–6}} * {{cite journal | author = Richards BD | year = 1918 | title = Food of bulbuls | journal = J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. | volume = 25 | issue = 3| page = 503 |url= https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/5454388}} * {{cite journal |vauthors=Shivnarayanan N, Naik RM | year = 1963 | title = Does the male redvented bulbul, ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (linnaeus) incubate? | journal = Pavo | volume = 1 | issue = 2| pages = 128–129}} * {{cite journal | author = Short LL | year = 1964 | title = Notes on the behaviour of the Bulbuls, ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Linnaeus) and ''P. leucogenys'' (Gray) in captivity | journal = Pavo | volume = 2 | issue = 1| pages = 26–36}} * {{cite book|author1=Thirumurthi, S |author2=Annamalai, R |author3=Gunasekaran, V |year=1993| chapter= Impact of stone crushing units on the populations of Redvented Bulbul, ''Pycnonotus cafer''| title=Bird Conservation: Strategies for the Nineties and Beyond| editor=Verghese, A| editor2=Sridhar, S| editor3=Chakravarthy, AK|publisher=Ornithological Society of India, Bangalore|pages=137–138|chapter-url=https://archive.org/stream/BirdConservationStrategies/BirdConservationIndia#page/n165/mode/1up/}} * Vijayan, VS. (1975) Ecological isolation of bulbuls (Family Pycnonotidae, Class Aves) with special reference to ''Pycnonotus cafer cafer'' (Linn.) and ''Pycnonotus luteolus luteolus'' (Lesson) at Point Calimere, Tamil Nadu. Ph.D. Dissertation, University of Bombay, Bombay. * {{cite journal | author = Watling D | year = 1983 | title = The breeding biology of the Red-vented Bulbul '' Pycnonotus cafer'' in Fiji | journal = Emu | volume = 83 | issue = 3| pages = 173–180 | doi = 10.1071/MU9830173| bibcode = 1983EmuAO..83..173W }} * {{cite journal | author = Watling D | year = 1986 | title = The timing of the moult in native and naturalised populations of the Red-vented Bulbul ''Pycnonotus cafer'' (Aves, Pycnonotidae) | journal = South Pacific J. Nat. Sci. | volume = 8 | pages = 104–124}} ==External links== * [http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/red-vented-bulbul-pycnonotus-cafer Red-vented bulbul videos, photos & sounds] on the Internet Bird Collection * [http://www.invasivespecies.net/database/species/ecology.asp?si=138&fr=1&sts= Invasive species database] * [http://www.biosecurity.govt.nz/pests/red-vented-bulbul New Zealand alert] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081014021111/http://biosecurity.govt.nz/pests/red-vented-bulbul |date=2008-10-14 }} * [http://www.hawaiiinvasivespecies.org/pests/redventedbulbul.html Hawaii] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081012104003/http://www.hawaiiinvasivespecies.org/pests/redventedbulbul.html |date=2008-10-12 }} {{Taxonbar|from=Q509455}} [[Category:Pycnonotus]] [[Category:Birds of South Asia]] [[Category:Birds of Myanmar]] [[Category:Birds of Fiji]] [[Category:Birds of Tonga]] [[Category:Birds of Samoa]] [[Category:Birds described in 1766]] [[Category:Animal taxa named by Carl Linnaeus]]

zh.extract:
黑喉红臀鹎（学名：Pycnonotus cafer）为鹎科鹎属的鸟类，俗名黑头公、红臀鹎。分布于巴基斯坦、印度、斯里兰卡、尼泊尔、锡金、不丹、孟加拉、中南半岛、爪哇以及中国大陆的四川、云南、贵州、广西、湖南、江西、福建、广东等地，多栖息于海拔约1900米以下开阔的山坡或平坝的次生阔叶林、灌木草丛、村寨庭园或村落附近的杂木林缘以及也见于季雨林、雨林或沟谷林缘。该物种的模式产地在斯里兰卡,至Baker时代。
國際自然保護聯盟物種存續委員會的入侵物種專家小組（ISSG）列為世界百大外來入侵種。


亚种
黑喉红臀鹎滇西亚种（学名：Pycnonotus cafer stanfordi）。分布于缅甸以及中国大陆的云南等地。该物种的模式产地在缅甸。
